findings.title: load() has no request-sequencing guard; overlapping invocations from markStatus() and the refresh button can let a stale response overwrite a fresher one
findings.description: load() (lines 53-70) is triggered from three independent call sites: onMounted (line 88), the refresh (刷新) button click (line 95), and markStatus() (line 77 and line 80, once per branch). The refresh button is guarded by disabled=loading, but the per-row action buttons (lines 140-153) are only guarded by updating.has(item.id), which is scoped to that row id, not to the global loading flag. That means: (1) a user can click refresh (loading=true, button disabled) and, while that load() is still in flight, click a different row check/cross button, which fires markStatus() -> updatePrStatus() -> its own independent load() call; or (2) a user can click check on row A and cross on row B in quick succession, producing two fully independent, concurrently-running load() chains (each doing app-config + fetchPrQueue). None of items.value, error.value, liteUnavailable.value, or loading.value are written with any awareness of which load() call is the latest one that should win -- each write is unconditional. Because fetchPrQueue() (frontend/src/api/prQueue.ts lines 38-47) is a bare fetch with no timeout, no AbortController, and no dedup, there is no bound on how out-of-order the two responses can arrive. If the earlier-started call network response happens to resolve after the later-started call response, its items.value assignment silently clobbers the fresher list -- e.g. showing an item pre-update status again right after the user just changed it, or reverting the whole table to a stale snapshot.
findings.concrete_inconsistency: Because error.value and liteUnavailable.value are only reset at the start of a given load() call (lines 55-56) and only re-set on that same call own completion, it is possible for one instance failure to set error.value after a different, later-resolving-but-earlier-started instance already populated items.value successfully and returned. blockState (lines 39-45) checks error.value before items.value.length, so the end state can be: items.value holds a valid, freshly-fetched list, but blockState still renders error (or vice versa: a stale liteUnavailable=true state left set after a subsequent successful non-LITE load already populated items). This is a genuine left in an inconsistent UI state case, not just a display of stale-but-self-consistent data.
findings.recommendation: Do not reach for a request library to fix this -- the fix is a request-generation token, not more dependencies. Increment a module-scoped (or component-scoped) counter at the top of load(), capture it in a local const token = ++loadToken, and before each side-effecting assignment (liteUnavailable.value, items.value, error.value, the finally loading.value=false) check if (token !== loadToken) return (or skip just that assignment). That is roughly 8-10 added lines and makes only the most recently started call response is allowed to win explicit, which is what the code currently assumes but never enforces. This also implicitly fixes the row-vs-row race in markStatus() since both underlying load() calls run through the same guarded function.

findings.title: No onUnmounted / AbortController cleanup for load(), inconsistent with how this codebase already solves the same class of problem elsewhere
findings.description: load() has no onUnmounted hook and no AbortController threaded through to fetchPrQueue()/getJson(), so a slow /app-config or /api/pr-queue response keeps running after the user navigates away from /app/pr-queue. Verified frontend/src/layout/AppShell.vue mounts a plain RouterView (no KeepAlive wrapper), so navigating away fully unmounts PrQueuePage component instance; because Vue 3 script-setup refs live in that instance own closure, a late-resolving promise writing into items.value/error.value/loading.value after unmount is inert rather than corrupting a live DOM node or a cached component -- this is meaningfully less severe than the same gap would be in a component that gets kept-alive or in a framework without per-instance closures. Still, it is a real inconsistency with how this same codebase already handles the identical class of problem: KeepAlivePage.vue (frontend/src/pages/KeepAlive/KeepAlivePage.vue lines 246-250) explicitly does onUnmounted clearing recheckTimer and republishTimer for its polling timers, and MonitorDashboard.vue (frontend/src/pages/Monitor/MonitorDashboard.vue) uses the vue-query package from tanstack (already a project dependency, not a new one) which handles request cancellation, staleness, and out-of-order responses for exactly this shape of fetch-on-mount-and-on-demand page. PrQueuePage was written by hand instead, and picked up the newer app-config check without picking up any of the surrounding guardrails.
findings.recommendation: If this page stays hand-rolled (reasonable, since it is simple), pair the load()-token fix above with a tiny disposed flag set in onUnmounted, and check disposed alongside the token before each state write -- a few more lines, still no new dependency. If the team wants the sturdier option, vue-query is already in the dependency tree via MonitorDashboard.vue, so adopting it here would not be pulling in more npm -- but that is a bigger rewrite than the bug warrants; start with the token guard.

residual_risks: fetchPrQueue() and updatePrStatus() (frontend/src/api/prQueue.ts) use raw fetch with no timeout/AbortController, unlike getJson()/sendJson() in frontend/src/api/client.ts which already carry a 15s AbortController-based timeout plus retry. A hung /api/pr-queue or /api/pr-queue/status response has no upper bound, which widens the window for the out-of-order-overwrite race above and means a truly stuck request never resolves the loading spinner for that call (mitigated only because a later, successfully-guarded load() will eventually reset loading.value, if the token-guard recommendation above is adopted).

markStatus() catch-then-load() (lines 78-80) calls load() again on failure to surface the error via load() per the inline comment, but load() unconditionally resets error.value to null at its own start (line 55) before attempting network requests again -- if that second load() itself fails on the app-config fetch (not fetchPrQueue), the resulting error is a generic one, not information about the original updatePrStatus() failure; this is a UX nit more than a race, kept here as low-priority context rather than a separate finding.

The button-level disabled=loading guard on the refresh button (line 95) relies on Vue microtask-based reactivity flush completing before the browser dispatches a second rapid click; this is reliable in practice in evergreen browsers but is an implicit assumption, not an enforced one -- the token-guard fix removes the need to rely on it.
testing_gaps: frontend/src/pages/PrQueue/PrQueuePage.spec.ts (4 tests, all added/adjacent to this diff) covers the new lite_edition branch in isolation but has no test for: (a) clicking refresh twice in quick succession with two mocked fetchPrQueue() calls resolving out of order, (b) markStatus() on two different rows firing concurrently, or (c) a load() in flight when the component unmounts. Given this diff added a brand-new piece of shared, overwritable state (liteUnavailable), a regression test asserting the response that resolves last is not always the one that wins if it started first would have caught the race described above.

No test exercises the per-row updating.value Set across two simultaneously-clicked rows to confirm the resulting items.value/error.value end state matches the last-started call rather than the last-resolved one.
